_comment: D331 PLAYABLE KENNESAW MOUNTAIN, June 27, 1864; D339 C71 INPUT CORRECTION. Single-phase Atlanta Campaign frontal-assault scenario built from the D330 spec. US attacks prepared Confederate high ground at Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw and Cheatham Hill / the Dead Angle. Sector identities and assault totals are Verified against NPS OOB, the NPS National Register text, and the NPS Pigeon Hill site bulletin; exact game-unit strengths are brigade/division-scale Inferred abstractions unless explicitly sourced. The complete Pigeon Hill commitment is the three named brigades totaling about 5,500; no separate fielded Morgan L. Smith reserve is added. No per-battle damage/firepower/casualty/winner fudge.

sources: NPS, Kennesaw Mountain National Battlefield Park overview
VMI Archives Historical Rosters, Alfred Jefferson Vaughan
NPS National Register text, Kennesaw Mountain National Battlefield Park
NPS, Federal Assault on Pigeon Hill site bulletin
American Battlefield Trust, Kennesaw Mountain battle page
American Battlefield Trust, Cheatham Hill article

weather.note: The June 27 assault is modeled as a daylight attack into visible fieldworks. Fog is off by default because the teaching point is terrain, breastworks, abatis, and command choice, not surprise.
sources: NPS Kennesaw Mountain National Battlefield Park overview
NPS Union and Confederate Orders of Battle
NPS National Register text, Kennesaw Mountain National Battlefield Park
American Battlefield Trust, Kennesaw Mountain and Cheatham Hill

kennesaw.name: Kennesaw Mountain - The Frontal Assault
kennesaw.place: Kennesaw Mountain, Georgia - Pigeon Hill, Little Kennesaw, and Cheatham Hill
kennesaw.blurb: After weeks of maneuver north of Atlanta, Sherman tries a direct blow against Johnston's prepared line. Logan's XV Corps attacks Pigeon Hill and Little Kennesaw; Thomas sends Newton and Davis against Cheatham's Hill and the Dead Angle. Dense timber, abatis, trenches, rocky slopes, and flanking guns turn the assault into a bloody repulse, while Schofield's wider movement becomes the campaign's real leverage.
kennesaw.provenance: Verified (high) for date, result, two-pronged assault shape, Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw and Cheatham Hill / Dead Angle sectors, principal commanders, named Union assault brigades, Confederate defending divisions, and casualty direction. Verified sector totals: about 5,500 Federals at Pigeon Hill across the complete three-brigade assault and about 9,000 Federals at Cheatham Hill. Inferred (medium) for per-brigade game strengths and exact battery groupings. D339 removes the unsupported extra 1,200-man Pigeon Hill reinforcement that had overfielded the verified commitment. Anti-Lost-Cause frame: Kennesaw was a Confederate tactical success but not proof that Johnston could save Atlanta by defensive genius alone; Sherman lost the frontal fight and still maneuvered the campaign forward.
kennesaw._assaultDoctrineNote: The AI Union attacker models the historically costly frontal assault into prepared works: it presses in line, absorbs fire, and does not get a modern perfectly timed bayonet solution by default. A human Union player can still coordinate a better attack. This is an input/doctrine choice, not an output fudge.

menu.title: KENNESAW MOUNTAIN (1864)
menu.deck: Sherman's costly frontal assault north of Atlanta - attack Pigeon Hill and the Dead Angle, or hold Johnston's fortified ridge line.
menu.aria: Kennesaw Mountain, June 1864 - the Atlanta Campaign frontal assault. Command the Union attack or the Confederate defense.

sides.heading: Kennesaw Mountain - Take Which Command?
sides.intro: June 27, 1864 - Sherman tests Johnston's prepared mountain line.

US.title: Command the Union assault
US.deck: Push Logan's and Thomas's assault columns through timber, abatis, and rocky slopes toward the ridge breastworks.

CS.title: Hold for the Confederacy
CS.deck: Anchor the ridge, hold the Dead Angle, and make the frontal assault pay for every yard of ground.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. Kennesaw is a single-phase battle; Schofield's flank movement appears in teaching, not as the playable objective.

brief.attack: Sherman has maneuvered Johnston toward Atlanta, but today he orders a direct assault. Strike both sectors: Pigeon Hill on the left and Cheatham's Hill at the Dead Angle. This is your war: history says the works and slopes break the attack; mass your brigades well enough to prove otherwise.
brief.defend: Hold the ridge line from Pigeon Hill to the Dead Angle. Your trenches, abatis, and guns are the battle. This is your war: stop the assault without pretending this tactical success can by itself save Atlanta.

objective.name: Kennesaw ridge breastworks and the Dead Angle
objective.note: The Confederate breastwork line spanning the two assault sectors. Holding this radius represents keeping Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw and Cheatham Hill / the Dead Angle connected as a defensive line. Historically the Union did not seize it.

leaders._note: Ranks are battle-date ranks. Johnston is still army commander; Hood is not. Sherman, Thomas, Schofield, McPherson, and Logan are major generals. Hardee is lieutenant general; Cheatham and Cleburne are major generals; Maney and Vaughan are brigadier generals.

US.name: Maj. Gen. William T. Sherman
US.note: Major General commanding the Military Division of the Mississippi. Ordered the June 27 frontal assault after weeks of maneuver. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. George H. Thomas
US.note: Major General commanding the Army of the Cumberland; chose the Cheatham Hill assault sector. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. John A. Logan
US.note: Major General commanding XV Corps; Brig. Gen. Morgan L. Smith's division furnishes two of the three Pigeon Hill assault brigades under Logan. Smith remains a command identity, not a separate fielded reserve echelon. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. James B. McPherson
US.note: Major General commanding the Army of the Tennessee; present at Kennesaw and killed later at Atlanta on July 22, not here. (Verified)

US.name: Brig. Gen. John Newton
US.note: Brigadier General commanding IV Corps Second Division in the Cheatham Hill assault. (Verified)

US.name: Brig. Gen. Jefferson C. Davis
US.note: Brigadier General commanding XIV Corps Second Division; do not confuse with the Confederate president or inflate rank. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. John M. Schofield
US.note: Major General commanding the Army of the Ohio. His far-right movement is campaign leverage, not the playable assault objective. (Verified)

CS.name: Gen. Joseph E. Johnston
CS.note: Full Confederate General commanding the Army of Tennessee at Kennesaw; relieved July 17, 1864. Hood is not yet army commander. (Verified)

CS.name: Lt. Gen. William J. Hardee
CS.note: Lieutenant General commanding the corps that includes Cheatham and Cleburne at the main assault sector. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Benjamin F. Cheatham
CS.note: Major General whose division anchors Cheatham Hill and the Dead Angle. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Patrick R. Cleburne
CS.note: Major General in Hardee's Corps; do not make him lieutenant general. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. William W. Loring
CS.note: Major General commanding Polk's old corps after Polk's death; sector context for the Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw end. (Verified)

US.name: Union trains - Burnt Hickory and Dallas roads

CS.name: Confederate ridge road and railroad rear

hills.name: Little Kennesaw Mountain
hills.note: Rocky high ground supporting the Pigeon Hill defense. (Verified)

hills.name: Pigeon Hill
hills.note: The XV Corps assault sector with dense underbrush, abatis, rocks, and steep approaches. (Verified)

hills.name: Cheatham's Hill
hills.note: The Army of the Cumberland assault sector, later named for Cheatham's defense. (Verified)

hills.name: Dead Angle salient
hills.note: The salient with trenches, traverses, abatis, and murderous close fire. (Verified)

hills.name: Big Kennesaw Mountain
hills.note: Campaign landmark and artillery context, not the playable assault objective. (Verified)

woods.note: Dense underbrush on the Pigeon Hill approach. (Verified)
Broken rocky slope and scrub around Little Kennesaw. (Verified)
Timber and abatis masking the Cheatham Hill approach before the open field. (Verified)
Right-sector woods near Davis's brigades and the Dallas Road approach. (Verified/Inferred)

walls.note: PIGEON HILL BREASTWORKS - Confederate works backed by rocky high ground and abatis. (Verified)
CHEATHAM HILL MAIN WORKS - the Confederate breastwork line around the Dead Angle. (Verified)
DEAD ANGLE TRAVERSES - interior trench/traverse line where Harker and McCook's men reached the works. (Verified)
PIGEON HILL ABATIS - felled timber and obstacles below the rocks. (Verified/Inferred placement)
CHEATHAM HILL ABATIS AND PINE STAKES - obstacle belt before the salient. (Verified/Inferred placement)

markers.name: Burnt Hickory Road
markers.note: Axis near the Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw assault. (Verified geography; inferred tactical path)

markers.name: Dallas Road
markers.note: Approach road tied to the Cheatham Hill / Dead Angle sector. (Verified/Inferred path)

markers.name: small creek below Cheatham Hill
markers.note: Creek and low ground between the assault line and the Confederate works. (Verified)

markers.name: Mebane's two-gun Tennessee Battery
markers.note: Two-gun battery/redoubt at the Dead Angle. (Verified)

markers.name: Pigeon Hill gorge and rock faces
markers.note: Steep broken ground that shattered XV Corps formation. (Verified)

markers.name: Schofield's flank movement
markers.note: Operational leverage that helped force Johnston's later withdrawal; not today's assault objective. (Verified)

markers.name: Western & Atlantic Railroad toward Atlanta
markers.note: Campaign context: Johnston defended the approaches to Atlanta's rail line. (Verified)

US.name: Giles A. Smith's Brigade
US.note: One of the three brigades in the complete 5,500-man Pigeon Hill assault; this game-scale split is inferred. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: Lightburn's Brigade
US.commander: Brig. Gen. Joseph A. J. Lightburn
US.note: One of two Morgan L. Smith division brigades in the complete 5,500-man Pigeon Hill assault. (Verified identity; Inferred strength within the 5,500 sector total)

US.name: Walcutt's Brigade
US.note: Harrow's division brigade in the complete 5,500-man Pigeon Hill assault; Walcutt was a colonel, not a general, at Kennesaw. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

US.name: Harker's Brigade, Newton's Division
US.note: Cheatham Hill assault brigade; Harker was mortally wounded at Kennesaw. Newton/Davis sector total about 9,000 Federals. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

US.name: Wagner's Brigade, Newton's Division
US.note: Named by the NPS National Register narrative among Newton's Cheatham Hill brigades. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: Kimball's Brigade, Newton's Division
US.note: Named Cheatham Hill assault brigade. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: McCook's Brigade, Davis's Division
US.note: McCook's brigade reached the Dead Angle; McCook was a colonel and was mortally wounded. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

US.name: Mitchell's Brigade, Davis's Division
US.note: Davis's other named attacking brigade at Cheatham Hill; Mitchell was a colonel. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

US.name: Union assault batteries
US.commander: Army of the Cumberland / Tennessee artillery
US.note: Aggregate field-gun support for the two-pronged assault. Universal gun model: 12 guns. (Inferred grouping)

CS.name: French's Division - Pigeon Hill Line
CS.note: Part of Loring's sector defending Little Kennesaw / Pigeon Hill in the National Register narrative. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Walker's Division - Little Kennesaw Line
CS.commander: Maj. Gen. W. H. T. Walker
CS.note: Defending division named for the Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw sector. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Maney's Tennessee Brigade
CS.note: Cheatham's Division, defending the Dead Angle. Maney was a brigadier general, not a major general. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Vaughan's Tennessee Brigade
CS.note: Cheatham's Division brigade in the NPS Confederate OOB. VMI Archives identifies Alfred Jefferson Vaughan; the NPS page uses a surname variant. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Cleburne's Division Detachment
CS.note: Hardee's Corps support on the Cheatham/Cleburne line. Cleburne was a major general. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Little Kennesaw Batteries
CS.note: Aggregate guns supporting the Pigeon Hill / Little Kennesaw defense. Universal gun model: 8 guns. (Verified artillery presence; Inferred grouping)

CS.name: Mebane's Tennessee Battery
CS.note: The two-gun battery/redoubt at the Dead Angle. Universal gun model: 2 guns. (Verified)

CS.name: Cheatham Hill Flanking Guns
CS.note: Aggregate flanking artillery around Cheatham Hill. Universal gun model: 6 guns. (Verified artillery role; Inferred grouping)

reinforcements.name: Cheatham Hill Reserve
reinforcements.entry: from the ridge road behind the Dead Angle
reinforcements.note: Reserve abstraction for the interior line and traverses around the salient. (Verified sector; Inferred strength/timing)

US.US: History is overturned. Your assault carried the Kennesaw ridge line that Sherman's men could not seize on June 27, 1864. In reality, the Union attack was repulsed with roughly three times Confederate losses, and Schofield's flank movement mattered more than the frontal blow.
US.CS: History is overturned against your army. The Union assault has carried the ridge line that Johnston's men historically held at Pigeon Hill and the Dead Angle.

CS.US: History holds. The ridge line and the Dead Angle remain in Confederate hands. The frontal assault failed, and the campaign's real movement came from Schofield's flank pressure after the repulse.
CS.CS: History holds. Johnston's army wins the tactical fight at Kennesaw: Pigeon Hill and the Dead Angle hold, the assault bleeds out below the works, and Sherman returns to maneuver.

endNote.draw: A bloodier stalemate than history: the works still stand, but the assault has torn enough of the ridge line to make Johnston's withdrawal feel closer.

teaching.title: Kennesaw Mountain - Prepared Works and the Cost of Frontal Assault

cards.head: Sherman tried a direct blow
cards.body: Kennesaw was a deliberate frontal assault after weeks of maneuver. The prepared Confederate line, steep ridges, abatis, trenches, and flanking guns made the attack a poor trade. The lesson is not that one side was braver; it is that 1864 field fortifications punished frontal attacks brutally.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - NPS Kennesaw overview; NPS National Register text; American Battlefield Trust.

cards.head: Two assault sectors, one lesson
cards.body: About 5,500 Federals attacked Pigeon Hill and about 9,000 attacked Cheatham Hill. The game splits those verified sector totals into named brigades but marks exact strengths as inferred. Both attacks met the same problem: obstacles and fire made the last yards almost impossible.
cards.provenance: Verified sector totals from the NPS National Register text and NPS Pigeon Hill site bulletin; brigade identities from NPS OOB and ABT.

cards.head: The Dead Angle
cards.body: At Cheatham Hill, men reached or nearly reached the works around the salient. Harker was killed, McCook was mortally wounded, and survivors dug close to the line. The name should carry human cost, not spectacle.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) for Harker and McCook casualties and the Dead Angle sector; exact game placement inferred.

cards.head: Tactical success, strategic withdrawal
cards.body: Johnston's army won the tactical fight on June 27, but Kennesaw did not save Atlanta. Schofield's movement on the Union right threatened the Confederate flank, and Johnston eventually withdrew. The battle shows the difference between winning a defensive fight and winning a campaign.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - NPS and ABT campaign framing.

codex.title: Kennesaw Mountain - June 27, 1864
codex.summary: Sherman's frontal assault against Johnston's prepared Kennesaw line failed at Pigeon Hill and Cheatham Hill / the Dead Angle. The Confederate tactical victory did not stop the Atlanta Campaign, because wider Union maneuver soon made the position untenable.
